claims.flip_evidence_text: (A) effect a Change of Recommendation (1) if an (x) unsolicited, bona fide written Acquisition Proposal is received by the Company and has not been withdrawn or (y) Intervening Event has occurred, and (2) the Company Board determines in good faith, after consultation with outside legal counsel, that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would be inconsistent with the directors’ fiduciary duties under applicable Law and, in the case of an Acquisition Proposal contemplated by clause (A)(1)(x) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii), after consultation with its financial advisor, that such Acquisition Proposal constitutes a Superior Proposal; and/or (B) cause or permit the Company or any of the Company’s Subsidiaries to enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Superior Proposal
claims.notes: Claim conflates distinct requirements for Change of Recommendation and Alternative Acquisition Agreement, omits the Intervening Event path (which does not require a Superior Proposal), and omits mandatory procedural preconditions (Notice Period, good-faith negotiation, etc.).

prompt:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
Section 6.02. Acquisition Proposals; Change of Recommendation. <omitted> (d) No Change of Recommendation or Alternative Acquisition Agreement. <omitted> (iii) Notwithstanding anything to the contrary set forth in this Section 6.02(d), prior to the Offer Acceptance Time, if there has not been a breach of the Company’s obligations set forth in this Section 6.02, the Company Board may: (A) effect a Change of Recommendation (1) if an (x) unsolicited, bona fide written Acquisition Proposal is received by the Company and has not been withdrawn or (y) Intervening Event has occurred, and (2) the Company Board determines in good faith, after consultation with outside legal counsel, -68- that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would be inconsistent with the directors’ fiduciary duties under applicable Law and, in the case of an Acquisition Proposal contemplated by clause (A)(1)(x) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii), after consultation with its financial advisor, that such Acquisition Proposal constitutes a Superior Proposal; and/or (B) cause or permit the Company or any of the Company’s Subsidiaries to enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Superior Proposal (and the Company may enter into or cause one of its Subsidiaries to enter into such an Alternative Acquisition Agreement) or agree, authorize or commit to do so; provided, however, that no such actions may be taken unless and until: (I) the Company has given Parent written notice at least four Business Days in advance (the “Notice Period”), which notice shall set forth in writing that the Company Board intends to consider whether to take such action and a reasonably detailed description of the basis therefor, and shall also include, (y) in the case of such an Acquisition Proposal, all information required by Section 6.02(c), mutatis mutandis, and (z) in the case of an Intervening Event, a reasonably detailed description of such Intervening Event; (II) during the Notice Period, to the extent requested by Parent, the Company shall, and shall cause its Representatives to, negotiate in good faith with Parent to revise this Agreement so that the conditions set forth in clauses (A)(2) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii) would not be satisfied or such Alternative Acquisition Agreement contemplated by clause (B) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii) would no longer be with respect to a Superior Proposal, as applicable; and (III) at the end of the Notice Period, the Company Board shall have taken into account any revisions to this Agreement proposed by Parent in writing in response to such notice contemplated by clause (I) of this 6.02(d)(iii) prior to the end of the Notice Period, and shall have thereafter determined in good faith, after consultation with outside legal counsel, that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would continue to be inconsistent with the directors’ fiduciary duties under applicable Law, or that such Alternative Acquisition Agreement contemplated by clause (B) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii), after consultation with its financial advisor, continues to be an Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Superior Proposal, as the case may be (it being understood that (y) any revisions to any Acquisition Proposal shall be deemed to be a new Acquisition Proposal for purposes of Section 6.02(c) and this Section 6.02(d)(iii), including for purposes of the Notice Period, except that subsequent to the initial Notice Period, the Notice Period shall be reduced to two Business Days and (z) prior to the Company or any of its Subsidiaries entering into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement contemplated by clause (B) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii), the Company shall have terminated this Agreement and abandoned the transactions contemplated by this Agreement pursuant to Section 8.03(b)). (Pages 73-74)

Question:
COR standard (superior offer)

Llama answer:
The Company Board may effect a Change of Recommendation or enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement if it determines in good faith that a failure to do so would be inconsistent with its fiduciary duties under applicable Law, and the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal.

Canonical claims:
- claim-e343c7dc82d7: The Company Board may effect a Change of Recommendation or enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement if it determines in good faith that a failure to do so would be inconsistent with its fiduciary duties under applicable Law, and the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal.

messages.content:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
Section 6.02. Acquisition Proposals; Change of Recommendation. <omitted> (d) No Change of Recommendation or Alternative Acquisition Agreement. <omitted> (iii) Notwithstanding anything to the contrary set forth in this Section 6.02(d), prior to the Offer Acceptance Time, if there has not been a breach of the Company’s obligations set forth in this Section 6.02, the Company Board may: (A) effect a Change of Recommendation (1) if an (x) unsolicited, bona fide written Acquisition Proposal is received by the Company and has not been withdrawn or (y) Intervening Event has occurred, and (2) the Company Board determines in good faith, after consultation with outside legal counsel, -68- that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would be inconsistent with the directors’ fiduciary duties under applicable Law and, in the case of an Acquisition Proposal contemplated by clause (A)(1)(x) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii), after consultation with its financial advisor, that such Acquisition Proposal constitutes a Superior Proposal; and/or (B) cause or permit the Company or any of the Company’s Subsidiaries to enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Superior Proposal (and the Company may enter into or cause one of its Subsidiaries to enter into such an Alternative Acquisition Agreement) or agree, authorize or commit to do so; provided, however, that no such actions may be taken unless and until: (I) the Company has given Parent written notice at least four Business Days in advance (the “Notice Period”), which notice shall set forth in writing that the Company Board intends to consider whether to take such action and a reasonably detailed description of the basis therefor, and shall also include, (y) in the case of such an Acquisition Proposal, all information required by Section 6.02(c), mutatis mutandis, and (z) in the case of an Intervening Event, a reasonably detailed description of such Intervening Event; (II) during the Notice Period, to the extent requested by Parent, the Company shall, and shall cause its Representatives to, negotiate in good faith with Parent to revise this Agreement so that the conditions set forth in clauses (A)(2) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii) would not be satisfied or such Alternative Acquisition Agreement contemplated by clause (B) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii) would no longer be with respect to a Superior Proposal, as applicable; and (III) at the end of the Notice Period, the Company Board shall have taken into account any revisions to this Agreement proposed by Parent in writing in response to such notice contemplated by clause (I) of this 6.02(d)(iii) prior to the end of the Notice Period, and shall have thereafter determined in good faith, after consultation with outside legal counsel, that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would continue to be inconsistent with the directors’ fiduciary duties under applicable Law, or that such Alternative Acquisition Agreement contemplated by clause (B) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii), after consultation with its financial advisor, continues to be an Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Superior Proposal, as the case may be (it being understood that (y) any revisions to any Acquisition Proposal shall be deemed to be a new Acquisition Proposal for purposes of Section 6.02(c) and this Section 6.02(d)(iii), including for purposes of the Notice Period, except that subsequent to the initial Notice Period, the Notice Period shall be reduced to two Business Days and (z) prior to the Company or any of its Subsidiaries entering into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement contemplated by clause (B) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii), the Company shall have terminated this Agreement and abandoned the transactions contemplated by this Agreement pursuant to Section 8.03(b)). (Pages 73-74)

Question:
COR standard (superior offer)

Llama answer:
The Company Board may effect a Change of Recommendation or enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement if it determines in good faith that a failure to do so would be inconsistent with its fiduciary duties under applicable Law, and the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal.

Canonical claims:
- claim-e343c7dc82d7: The Company Board may effect a Change of Recommendation or enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement if it determines in good faith that a failure to do so would be inconsistent with its fiduciary duties under applicable Law, and the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal.

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to act as a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. I need to evaluate a claim against a contract excerpt, a question, and a model answer (Llama answer). 

 The claim is: 
 "The Company Board may effect a Change of Recommendation or enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement if it determines in good faith that a failure to do so would be inconsistent with its fiduciary duties under applicable Law, and the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal."

 Let me analyze the contract excerpt carefully.

 From Section 6.02(d)(iii):
 The Company Board may:
 (A) effect a Change of Recommendation (1) if an (x) unsolicited, bona fide written Acquisition Proposal is received... or (y) Intervening Event has occurred, AND (2) the Company Board determines in good faith, after consultation with outside legal counsel, that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would be inconsistent with the directors' fiduciary duties under applicable Law and, in the case of an Acquisition Proposal... that such Acquisition Proposal constitutes a Superior Proposal; and/or
 (B) cause or permit the Company... to enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Superior Proposal...

 So for (A) Change of Recommendation:
 - Needs unsolicited, bona fide written Acquisition Proposal OR Intervening Event
 - AND determination that failure to change would be inconsistent with fiduciary duties
 - AND in case of Acquisition Proposal, determination that it's a Superior Proposal (after consultation with financial advisor)

 For (B) Alternative Acquisition Agreement:
 - Needs to be with respect to a Superior Proposal
 - But the claim says "if it determines in good faith that a failure to do so would be inconsistent with its fiduciary duties under applicable Law, and the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal."

 Wait, for entering into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement (B), does the text explicitly say the Board must determine that failure to do so would be inconsistent with fiduciary duties? Let me check.

 Clause (B) says: "cause or permit the Company or any of the Company’s Subsidiaries to enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Superior Proposal"

 The conditions in (I), (II), (III) apply to both (A) and (B). Looking at (III): "at the end of the Notice Period, the Company Board shall have taken into account any revisions... and shall have thereafter determined in good faith, after consultation with outside legal counsel, that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would continue to be inconsistent with the directors' fiduciary duties under applicable Law, OR that such Alternative Acquisition Agreement... continues to be an Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Superior Proposal, as the case may be"

 Hmm, the claim combines elements. Let me parse the claim exactly:
 "The Company Board may effect a Change of Recommendation or enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement if it determines in good faith that a failure to do so would be inconsistent with its fiduciary duties under applicable Law, and the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal."

 Issues with the claim:
 1. For Change of Recommendation based on an Intervening Event, the Board does NOT need the Acquisition Proposal to constitute a Superior Proposal (since there is no Acquisition Proposal in that case). The claim implies that for ANY Change of Recommendation or Alternative Acquisition Agreement, the Board must determine both fiduciary inconsistency AND Superior Proposal. But for Change of Recommendation due to Intervening Event, only fiduciary duty inconsistency is required.

 2. For entering into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement, the text says "with respect to a Superior Proposal" but doesn't explicitly state in clause (B) itself that the Board must determine fiduciary duty inconsistency. However, looking at the overall structure, clause (A)(2) requires the fiduciary determination. Clause (B) is about entering into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement. The claim says both actions require BOTH fiduciary determination AND Superior Proposal status.

 Actually, let me look more carefully. The claim says the Board may effect a Change of Recommendation OR enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement if:
 - it determines in good faith that failure to do so would be inconsistent with fiduciary duties
 - AND the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal.

 But the contract says:
 For Change of Recommendation (A):
 - (1)(x) unsolicited bona fide Acquisition Proposal OR (y) Intervening Event
 - (2) determines failure to effect Change of Recommendation inconsistent with fiduciary duties AND, in case of Acquisition Proposal, that it constitutes Superior Proposal

 So if it's an Intervening Event, the Board can change recommendation without a Superior Proposal.

 For Alternative Acquisition Agreement (B):
 - Must be with respect to a Superior Proposal
 - The Notice Period and conditions in (III) say at the end the Board determines either that failure to effect Change of Recommendation continues to be inconsistent with fiduciary duties OR that the Alternative Acquisition Agreement continues to be with respect to a Superior Proposal. Wait, for Alternative Acquisition Agreement, is the fiduciary duty determination required? Let me read (III) again carefully.

 (III): "at the end of the Notice Period, the Company Board shall have taken into account any revisions... and shall have thereafter determined in good faith, after consultation with outside legal counsel, that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would continue to be inconsistent with the directors' fiduciary duties under applicable Law, or that such Alternative Acquisition Agreement contemplated by clause (B) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii), after consultation with its financial advisor, continues to be an Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Superior Proposal, as the case may be"

 "as the case may be" suggests:
 - If taking action under (A) (Change of Recommendation), determine fiduciary inconsistency
 - If taking action under (B) (Alternative Acquisition Agreement), determine it continues to be with respect to a Superior Proposal

 Actually, looking at the structure, for (B), the requirement is that it is "with respect to a Superior Proposal". But does the Board need to make a fiduciary duty determination specifically for entering into the Alternative Acquisition Agreement? The text in (III) says "as the case may be" connecting to two different determinations depending on which action is taken.

 However, the claim says both actions require BOTH fiduciary determination AND Superior Proposal. This seems incorrect because:
 1. Change of Recommendation can be based on Intervening Event without Superior Proposal.
 2. The claim might be inaccurate about the Alternative Acquisition Agreement requiring a fiduciary duty determination specifically in the way stated, though one could argue entering into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement requires it to be a Superior Proposal, and the Change of Recommendation requires fiduciary duty.

 But wait - the Llama answer and the claim are essentially the same. The question is "COR standard (superior offer)". The claim is likely extracted from the Llama answer.

 Let me assess correctness:
 - The claim says the Board may do these things if it determines (1) fiduciary inconsistency AND (2) Superior Proposal.
 - But the contract allows Change of Recommendation for an Intervening Event without a Superior Proposal.
 - Also, the contract allows Change of Recommendation for an Acquisition Proposal if it's a Superior Proposal AND fiduciary inconsistency.
 - For Alternative Acquisition Agreement, it must be a Superior Proposal.

 So the claim is partially true but false/misleading because:
 - It omits the requirement that the Acquisition Proposal must be unsolicited and bona fide (for the Change of Recommendation based on a proposal).
 - It incorrectly states that Superior Proposal is required for ALL Change of Recommendations (intervening events don't require this).
 - It implies both conditions apply to both actions in the same way.

 Actually, looking at the claim again: "The Company Board may effect a Change of Recommendation or enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement if it determines in good faith that a failure to do so would be inconsistent with its fiduciary duties under applicable Law, and the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal."

 This is definitely not fully accurate. It overstates the requirement for Superior Proposal (doesn't apply to Intervening Event changes) and possibly misstates the requirements for Alternative Acquisition Agreement (which must be a Superior Proposal, but the fiduciary duty determination language in the claim is tied to the Change of Recommendation action).

 Wait, is there any requirement of fiduciary duty determination for entering into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement? Looking at (B): "cause or permit the Company or any of the Company’s Subsidiaries to enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Superior Proposal" - there's no explicit "determines in good faith that failure to do so would be inconsistent with fiduciary duties" in clause (B) itself. However, the notice period condition (III) requires for the Board to determine at the end that either failure to effect Change of Recommendation continues to be inconsistent with fiduciary duties OR the Alternative Acquisition Agreement continues to be with respect to a Superior Proposal. 

 So for Alternative Acquisition Agreement, the Board must determine it continues to be a Superior Proposal, not necessarily that failure to enter would be inconsistent with fiduciary duties. Though arguably entering into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement might implicitly require fiduciary duty, the text specifically links the fiduciary duty determination to the Change of Recommendation.

 Therefore, the claim is partially true at best, and arguably false because:
 1. It says Change of Recommendation requires Superior Proposal always (false due to Intervening Event exception).
 2. It says Alternative Acquisition Agreement requires fiduciary duty determination (not clearly stated; the "as the case may be" suggests one or the other depending on the action).
 3. It omits other key conditions (unsolicited, bona fide, written, notice period, negotiation, etc.).

 However, in these annotation tasks, we need to determine if the claim is "true", "false", or "partially_true". Given that it captures some elements but misses critical distinctions (Intervening Event exception, the fact that Alternative Acquisition Agreement doesn't explicitly require the fiduciary finding in the same way), I think "partially_true" is appropriate. But wait - the question is "COR standard (superior offer)" which might focus on the superior offer aspect.

 Let me reconsider. Is the claim "load bearing"? The claim is essentially the core of the Llama answer. The question asks about the COR standard (superior offer). The claim seems to be the main content of the answer. So load_bearing_label should be "yes". The flip evidence would be text showing the correct standard, particularly the Intervening Event exception or the detailed requirements.

 Actually, looking at the fixed claim list, there's only one claim, and it matches the Llama answer almost exactly. The task is to evaluate this specific claim.

 For "correctness_label":
 The claim is a simplified summary. Is it partially true or false? 
 - It correctly states that for a Change of Recommendation based on an Acquisition Proposal, the Board must determine fiduciary inconsistency AND that it's a Superior Proposal.
 - It correctly states that an Alternative Acquisition Agreement must constitute a Superior Proposal.
 - But it fails to mention the Intervening Event exception where no Superior Proposal is needed.
 - It conflates the requirements for the two actions.

 In many legal annotation schemes, if a claim is accurate for the primary scenario but misses an exception, it might be "partially_true". However, given that the Intervening Event is a significant alternative path for Change of Recommendation, saying it ALWAYS requires a Superior Proposal is false. 

 But let me think about the context. The question is "COR standard (superior offer)". This suggests the question is specifically about the standard when there's a superior offer, not an intervening event. In that context, the claim might be considered true regarding the superior offer standard. However, the claim itself says "The Company Board may effect a Change of Recommendation or enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement if..." which is a general statement about when these actions may be taken, not qualified as "in the case of a superior offer".

 Given the precise nature of legal texts, I think "partially_true" is the safest label because the claim is accurate in part but inaccurate in that it omits the Intervening Event path and overstates the uniformity of the requirements. But actually, some annotators might mark it "false" because it states requirements that aren't fully accurate as a universal rule. 

 Wait, I should check if the claim says "the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal". For an Intervening Event, there is no Acquisition Proposal. So the claim's condition "the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal" wouldn't apply because there's no Acquisition Proposal in the Intervening Event scenario. The claim is saying these are the conditions for the Board to take action. Since the Board can take action (Change of Recommendation) without any Acquisition Proposal at all (via Intervening Event), the claim is false as a universal statement.

 However, looking at the Llama answer and the claim side by side, they are identical. The question is "COR standard (superior offer)" implying the context is about a superior offer. But the claim doesn't limit itself to that context.

 Let me look at what "COR" means - Change of Recommendation. So the question is asking about the standard for Change of Recommendation when there's a superior offer. The Llama answer addresses both Change of Recommendation and Alternative Acquisition Agreement.

 If the question is specifically about COR standard (superior offer), then the claim should focus on the Acquisition Proposal scenario. In that scenario, the claim is mostly true BUT it omits:
 - The proposal must be unsolicited, bona fide, and written
 - The Board must consult outside legal counsel and financial advisor
 - The 4-day notice period and negotiation requirements
 - The termination requirement before entering Alternative Acquisition Agreement

 Is the claim "true" despite omitting procedural requirements? Usually, in these annotation tasks, if the claim states a conditional ("if") and the conditions mentioned are correct but incomplete, it might be considered "partially_true" or "true" depending on whether the omissions are material. But the claim says "if it determines... and... constitutes a Superior Proposal." This suggests these are the ONLY conditions, which is false because there are many other conditions (notice period, good faith negotiation, etc.).

 Actually, looking at similar annotation guidelines for legal claims, if a claim states a rule but omits significant conditions or exceptions, it's typically "partially_true" or "false". Given that this claim misses the Intervening Event exception and numerous procedural requirements, and conflates the fiduciary duty requirement (which applies to Change of Recommendation) with the Superior Proposal requirement (which applies to both but differently), I will label it "partially_true".

 Wait, I need to reconsider the load_bearing aspect. The claim is the entire Llama answer. So it's definitely load bearing.

 Flip evidence: I need to provide text from the contract that shows the correct standard or contradicts the claim. The key flip evidence would be the text showing:
 1. The Intervening Event exception: "(y) Intervening Event has occurred, and (2) the Company Board determines in good faith, after consultation with outside legal counsel, -68- that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would be inconsistent with the directors’ fiduciary duties under applicable Law and, in the case of an Acquisition Proposal contemplated by clause (A)(1)(x) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii), after consultation with its financial advisor, that such Acquisition Proposal constitutes a Superior Proposal"
 2. The procedural requirements: notice period, negotiation, etc.

 Actually, for the flip evidence, I should quote the text that demonstrates the claim is incomplete or incorrect. The most relevant part is the distinction between actions (A) and (B) and the Intervening Event.

 Let me draft:
 correctness_label: partially_true
 load_bearing_label: yes
 flip_evidence_text: "(iii) Notwithstanding anything to the contrary set forth in this Section 6.02(d), prior to the Offer Acceptance Time, if there has not been a breach of the Company’s obligations set forth in this Section 6.02, the Company Board may: (A) effect a Change of Recommendation (1) if an (x) unsolicited, bona fide written Acquisition Proposal is received by the Company and has not been withdrawn or (y) Intervening Event has occurred, and (2) the Company Board determines in good faith, after consultation with outside legal counsel, that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would be inconsistent with the directors’ fiduciary duties under applicable Law and, in the case of an Acquisition Proposal contemplated by clause (A)(1)(x) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii), after consultation with its financial advisor, that such Acquisition Proposal constitutes a Superior Proposal; and/or (B) cause or permit the Company or any of the Company’s Subsidiaries to enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Superior Proposal"

 This flip evidence shows that:
 1. Change of Recommendation can be based on Intervening Event without Superior Proposal
 2. The fiduciary duty determination is specific to Change of Recommendation, while Alternative Acquisition Agreement requires being with respect to a Superior Proposal
 3. The proposal must be unsolicited, bona fide, and written

 Actually, the flip evidence should probably be shorter and focused. But the instructions say "flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes". It doesn't specify length. I'll provide the key contrasting text.

 Wait, looking again at the contract excerpt provided, the page numbers are included in the text "(Pages 73-74)". I should probably not include that in the quote.

 Let me reconsider if the claim might be "false". The claim states a rule that combines conditions. It says the Board may effect a Change of Recommendation "if" certain conditions are met. But the Board may also effect a Change of Recommendation based on an Intervening Event without a Superior Proposal. So the claim's "if" is too restrictive - it says the Board may do X if P and Q, but actually the Board may do X if P and Q, OR if R (where R is Intervening Event). This makes the claim false as a statement of the conditions under which the Board may act. 

 However, in the context of the question "COR standard (superior offer)", the claim is answering about the superior offer scenario. But the claim itself is phrased as a general statement of when the Board may act. 

 I think "partially_true" is appropriate because it gets the fiduciary duty and Superior Proposal aspects right for the primary scenario (superior offer) but is incomplete/wrong about it being the exclusive path.

 Actually, you know what, looking at this again: The claim says "The Company Board may effect a Change of Recommendation or enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement if it determines in good faith that a failure to do so would be inconsistent with its fiduciary duties under applicable Law, and the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal."

 The phrase "failure to do so" is ambiguous. Does "do so" refer to "effect a Change of Recommendation or enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement"? If so, for Alternative Acquisition Agreement, the text doesn't explicitly say the Board must determine that failure to enter would be inconsistent with fiduciary duties. The text says the Board may enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement "with respect to a Superior Proposal". The fiduciary duty language is in clause (A)(2) for Change of Recommendation. So the claim is also wrong in attributing the fiduciary duty determination to Alternative Acquisition Agreement.

 Given these issues, I think "partially_true" is the right label. It captures some correct elements but misses important distinctions and exceptions.

 Let me check if there's any reason to call it "true". If we're being very charitable and assuming the claim is only about the superior offer context (given the question), then yes, the Board must determine fiduciary inconsistency and that it's a Superior Proposal. But the claim explicitly mentions "enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement" and says "the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal". An Alternative Acquisition Agreement doesn't "constitute" a Superior Proposal - it is entered into "with respect to a Superior Proposal". This is a minor semantic point.

 I think the best answer is:
 correctness_label: partially_true
 load_bearing_label: yes
 flip_evidence_text: [the text showing the Intervening Event exception and the separate requirements for (A) and (B)]
 notes: Claim omits the Intervening Event exception and other procedural requirements; incorrectly suggests fiduciary duty determination applies identically to both actions.

 Actually, looking at the exact text of the claim and the contract, the claim is very close to the Llama answer. The question asks about "COR standard (superior offer)". The canonical claim is exactly the Llama answer. In many of these datasets, if the answer captures the gist but is incomplete, it's "partially_true". If it's the main claim of the answer, it's load bearing.

 Let me finalize.

 correctness_label: partially_true
 load_bearing_label: yes
 flip_evidence_text: "(A) effect a Change of Recommendation (1) if an (x) unsolicited, bona fide written Acquisition Proposal is received by the Company and has not been withdrawn or (y) Intervening Event has occurred, and (2) the Company Board determines in good faith, after consultation with outside legal counsel, that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would be inconsistent with the directors’ fiduciary duties under applicable Law and, in the case of an Acquisition Proposal contemplated by clause (A)(1)(x) of this Section 6.02(d)(iii), after consultation with its financial advisor, that such Acquisition Proposal constitutes a Superior Proposal; and/or (B) cause or permit the Company or any of the Company’s Subsidiaries to enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Superior Proposal"
 notes: The claim conflates distinct requirements for Change of Recommendation and Alternative Acquisition Agreement, omits the Intervening Event path (which does not require a Superior Proposal), and omits procedural preconditions such as the Notice Period and good-faith negotiation.

 This looks solid.

reasoning_details.text:
The user wants me to act as a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. I need to evaluate a claim against a contract excerpt, a question, and a model answer (Llama answer). 

 The claim is: 
 "The Company Board may effect a Change of Recommendation or enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement if it determines in good faith that a failure to do so would be inconsistent with its fiduciary duties under applicable Law, and the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal."

 Let me analyze the contract excerpt carefully.

 From Section 6.02(d)(iii):
 The Company Board may:
 (A) effect a Change of Recommendation (1) if an (x) unsolicited, bona fide written Acquisition Proposal is received... or (y) Intervening Event has occurred, AND (2) the Company Board determines in good faith, after consultation with outside legal counsel, that a failure to effect a Change of Recommendation would be inconsistent with the directors' fiduciary duties under applicable Law and, in the case of an Acquisition Proposal... that such Acquisition Proposal constitutes a Superior Proposal; and/or
 (B) cause or permit the Company... to enter into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement with respect to a Superior Proposal...

 So for (A) Change of Recommendation:
 - Needs unsolicited, bona fide written Acquisition Proposal OR Intervening Event
 - AND determination that failure to change would be inconsistent with fiduciary duties
 - AND in case of Acquisition Proposal, determination that it's a Superior Proposal (after consultation with financial advisor)

 For (B) Alternative Acquisition Agreement:
 - Needs to be with respect to a Superior Proposal
 - But the claim says "if it determines in good faith that a failure to do so would be inconsistent with its fiduciary duties under applicable Law, and the Acquisition Proposal or Alternative Acquisition Agreement constitutes a Superior Proposal."

 Wait, for entering into an Alternative Acquisition Agreement (B), does the text explicitly say the Board must determine that failure to do so would be inconsistent with fiduciary duties? Let me check.
